draft.introduction: The prayer to Isis in Book XI of Apuleius’s Metamorphoses (c. 170 CE) is the climactic moment of the novel, where Lucius, transformed into a donkey, prays to the goddess who restores his humanity. It is the fullest literary account of Isiac devotion in Latin.

translation.text: O holy and eternal guardian of the human race, you who always cherish mortals and bless them, you care for the troubles of miserable humans with a sweet mother’s love. Neither a day nor a night nor even a brief moment passes empty of your blessings.
translation.original_text: O sancta et humani generis sospitatrix perpetua, quae semper foves mortales, dulcis adfectio matris blanditur. Nec dies nec quies nec momentum ullum tuis transcurrit beneficiis vacuum.

translator_notes.note: Apuleius’s Isis prayer is the closest parallel in pagan literature to Marian devotion. The language of maternal love, perpetual guardianship, and universal salvation closely anticipates later Christian hymns to the Virgin Mary, fueling scholarly debate about whether Isiac worship directly influenced Marian theology.
